parts.content: Describe the change in pH during the titration of a weak acid with a strong base.
parts.markscheme:
- pH increases gradually at first, with a buffer region where pH rises slowly
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Then a sharp rise near equivalence, ending with pH > 7 due to excess strong base
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Initial pH and slow rise
steps.content: At the start of the titration, the flask contains only ethanoic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$). Since it is a **weak acid**, it only partially dissociates, resulting in an initial low pH (typically around 2.8–3.0). As we begin adding sodium hydroxide ($\mathrm{NaOH}$), the pH initially increases at a relatively slow rate as the acid starts to be neutralized.

steps.title: Formation of the buffer region
steps.content:
As more strong base is added, some of the ethanoic acid is converted into its conjugate base, the ethanoate ion ($\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$):

$$\mathrm{CH_3COOH(aq) + OH^-(aq) \rightarrow CH_3COO^-(aq) + H_2O(l)}$$

This creates a **buffer region** where both the weak acid and its conjugate base are present in significant concentrations. In this phase, the pH rises very slowly because the buffer resists changes in pH upon the addition of small amounts of $\mathrm{OH^-}$. This concept is covered in **Topic R3.1: Proton transfer reactions**.

highlights.text: pH increases gradually at first, with a buffer region where pH rises slowly

steps.title: Sharp rise at equivalence
steps.content:
As we approach the **equivalence point** (where the moles of base added equal the initial moles of acid), the buffering capacity is exhausted. This leads to a **sharp vertical increase** in pH. 

For a weak acid–strong base titration, the equivalence point occurs at a **pH > 7**. This is because the resulting ethanoate ions react with water (hydrolysis) to produce hydroxide ions:

$$\mathrm{CH_3COO^-(aq) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons CH_3COOH(aq) + OH^-(aq)}$$

highlights.text: Then a sharp rise near equivalence, ending with pH > 7 due to excess strong base

steps.title: Final alkaline plateau
steps.content: Beyond the equivalence point, the pH change slows down again. The curve forms an **alkaline plateau** as the pH is now determined by the concentration of the excess strong base ($\mathrm{NaOH}$). The pH will eventually level off, approaching the pH of the $0.100 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ sodium hydroxide solution used in the burette.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the pH of 0.100 M NaOH using pH = 14 + log10([OH-]).

ti84.instructions: To find the pOH, calculate -log(0.100). Then subtract the result from 14 to find the pH.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the pH of the excess strong base.

calculator.description: Calculate the theoretical maximum pH of the titration using the concentration of the strong base.

steps.title: Analyze initial species
steps.content:
Initially, the flask contains only ethanoic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$), which is a **weak acid**. This means it only partially dissociates in water according to the equilibrium:

$$\mathrm{CH_3COOH(aq) \rightleftharpoons CH_3COO^-(aq) + H^+(aq)}$$

Because the concentration of $\mathrm{H^+}$ ions is relatively low compared to a strong acid, the starting pH is low (usually between 2 and 3) but not as low as that of a strong acid like $\mathrm{HCl}$.

steps.title: Formation of the buffer
steps.content:
As $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is added, it reacts with the ethanoic acid to produce sodium ethanoate and water:

$$\mathrm{CH_3COOH(aq) + OH^-(aq) \rightarrow CH_3COO^-(aq) + H_2O(l)}$$

In this region, both the weak acid ($\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$) and its conjugate base ($\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$) are present in the solution. This creates a **buffer system**, which resists changes in pH. Consequently, the **pH increases gradually** and slowly as more base is added.

steps.title: Visualize the pH curve
steps.content: To better understand this behavior, you can use a graphing calculator to see how the pH changes as the volume of base increases. This helps identify the "buffer region" and the "equivalence point."

gdc.instructions: Graph the pH curve. Note the 'half-equivalence point' where pH = pKa. The curve will be relatively flat in this region due to buffering.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. To see the general shape of a titration curve, you can plot an example function or use the 'Table' feature if you have data points.
3. Press [GRAPH] and use [TRACE] to find the equivalence point where the slope is steepest.

desmos.instructions: Search for 'Acid-Base Titration Curve' templates. Observe how changing the Ka of the acid shifts the starting pH and the pH at the equivalence point.

calculator.description: Graph the pH curve for a weak acid-strong base titration. While the exact equation is complex, you can approximate the shape using the Henderson-Hasselbalch relationship for the buffer region.

steps.title: Equivalence and salt hydrolysis
steps.content:
At the **equivalence point**, all the ethanoic acid has been neutralized. The main chemical species present is the ethanoate ion ($\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$). 

Crucially, the ethanoate ion is a conjugate base that undergoes **hydrolysis** with water:

$$\mathrm{CH_3COO^-(aq) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons CH_3COOH(aq) + OH^-(aq)}$$

Because this reaction produces $\mathrm{OH^-}$ ions, the **pH at equivalence is greater than 7**. Near this point, there is a **sharp rise** in pH.

steps.title: Final pH with excess base
steps.content: After the equivalence point, any further addition of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ means there is an excess of hydroxide ions in the solution. Since $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is a **strong base**, it dissociates completely. The pH continues to rise and eventually levels off at a high value (around 12–13), dictated by the concentration of the excess strong base.

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks, your description should include:
1. The **gradual pH increase** at the start due to the formation of a **buffer region** (where the weak acid and its conjugate base coexist).
2. The **sharp rise** near the equivalence point, resulting in a **pH > 7** because of the hydrolysis of the conjugate base salt.

parts.content: Identify a suitable indicator for this titration and justify your choice with reference to the pH at the equivalence point.
parts.markscheme:
- Indicator: Phenolphthalein
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Colour change occurs in pH range 8–10, matching equivalence point of weak acid/strong base titration
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Classify the titration pair
steps.content:
To choose the right indicator, we first need to identify the nature of the reactants. The question identifies the two substances for us:

*   **Ethanoic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$):** A weak acid.
*   **Sodium hydroxide ($\mathrm{NaOH}$):** A strong base.

This is a **weak acid–strong base titration**. Understanding this classification is the first step in predicting where the equivalence point will lie on the pH scale.

highlights.text: ethanoic acid is a weak acid
sodium hydroxide is a strong base

steps.title: Determine equivalence point pH
steps.content:
In a titration between a weak acid and a strong base, the salt formed at the equivalence point (sodium ethanoate, $\mathrm{CH_3COONa}$) undergoes hydrolysis. 

The ethanoate ion ($\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$) reacts with water to produce hydroxide ions ($\mathrm{OH^-}$):
$$\mathrm{CH_3COO^-(aq) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons CH_3COOH(aq) + OH^-(aq)}$$

Because of the production of $\mathrm{OH^-}$ ions, the **pH at the equivalence point is greater than 7** (typically between pH 8 and 10).

steps.title: Select the suitable indicator
steps.content:
An appropriate indicator must have a color change range that overlaps with the vertical region of the titration curve, which includes the pH at the equivalence point. 

Since our equivalence point is basic ($pH > 7$), **phenolphthalein** is the best choice. Its pH range for color change is approximately **8.3 to 10.0**, which perfectly matches the expected equivalence point of this weak acid–strong base pair.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To get full marks on this question, you must state both the indicator and the justification:

1.  **Indicator:** Phenolphthalein.
2.  **Justification:** The equivalence point of a weak acid–strong base titration occurs at a basic pH ($pH > 7$), which falls within the color change range of phenolphthalein (8–10).

steps.title: Analyze the reactants
steps.content:
First, we identify the strength of our reactants. The question states we are titrating **ethanoic acid**, which is a **weak acid**, with **sodium hydroxide**, which is a **strong base**. 

In a titration between a weak acid and a strong base, the equivalence point is not neutral ($	ext{pH} = 7$) because of the nature of the salt produced. This concept is explored in **Reactivity 3.1: Proton transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Identify salt formation
steps.content:
At the equivalence point, the acid and base have reacted completely. The neutralization reaction is:

$$\mathrm{CH_3COOH(aq) + NaOH(aq) \rightarrow CH_3COONa(aq) + H_2O(l)}$$

The salt formed is **sodium ethanoate** ($\mathrm{CH_3COONa}$). In the solution, this salt dissociates into sodium ions ($\mathrm{Na}^+$) and ethanoate ions ($\mathrm{CH_3COO}^-$).

steps.title: Apply salt hydrolysis reasoning
steps.content:
The ethanoate ion ($\mathrm{CH_3COO}^-$) is the conjugate base of a weak acid. Because it is a relatively strong conjugate base, it reacts with water in a process called **hydrolysis**:

$$\mathrm{CH_3COO^-(aq) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons CH_3COOH(aq) + OH^-(aq)}$$

The production of hydroxide ions ($\mathrm{OH}^-$) increases the pH of the solution. Therefore, at the equivalence point, the solution is **basic** ($\text{pH} > 7$).

steps.title: Select the suitable indicator
steps.content:
Since the equivalence point is basic (typically around $\text{pH} \approx 8.7$ for this specific titration), we must choose an indicator that changes color in the basic range.

**Phenolphthalein** is the suitable indicator because its color change occurs between **pH 8.2 and 10.0**, which matches the expected pH at the equivalence point.

parts.content: Sketch a pH curve for this titration. Label the buffer region and equivalence point clearly.
parts.markscheme:
![Titration Curve](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/6f065c80-4243-44cd-99b6-1738138f621d.png)

- Curve shows gradual increase $\rightarrow$ buffer region $\rightarrow$ sharp vertical rise $\rightarrow$ levels off
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Buffer region and equivalence point clearly labeled
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify titration type
steps.content:
To sketch the correct pH curve, we first identify the strength of the reactants. The question states we are titrating a **weak acid** with a **strong base**. 

*   **Acid:** Ethanoic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$), which is a weak acid.
*   **Base:** Sodium hydroxide ($\mathrm{NaOH}$), which is a strong base.

This specific combination results in a characteristic 'S' shaped curve that starts at a moderate pH and ends at a high pH.

highlights.text: ethanoic acid is a weak acid
sodium hydroxide is a strong base

steps.title: Determine starting pH and buffer
steps.content:
Since we start with a weak acid, the initial pH will not be extremely low (usually between 3 and 4). 

As we add small amounts of $\mathrm{NaOH}$, some ethanoic acid is converted into its conjugate base (ethanoate ions). This creates a **buffer region** where the solution resists large changes in pH. On your graph, this appears as a relatively flat section early in the titration.

steps.title: Identify the equivalence point
steps.content:
In a weak acid-strong base titration, the salt formed at the equivalence point (sodium ethanoate) undergoes hydrolysis, making the solution slightly basic. 

Therefore, the **equivalence point** must be labeled on the vertical section of the curve at a **pH > 7**.

steps.title: Sketch and label curve
steps.content:
Combine these qualitative features into your sketch:
1.  **Start:** pH $\approx 3-4$.
2.  **Buffer Region:** A gentle slope following an initial small jump.
3.  **Equivalence Point:** The middle of the steep vertical section, located above pH 7.
4.  **Final Plateau:** The curve levels off at a high pH (around 12-13) as excess strong base is added.

$$\text{Final Curve Profile: } \text{Gradual rise} \rightarrow \text{Buffer} \rightarrow \text{Vertical rise (pH > 7)} \rightarrow \text{Level off}$$

steps.title: Identify the titration type
steps.content:
First, we identify that this is a titration between a **weak acid** (ethanoic acid) and a **strong base** (sodium hydroxide). 

This tells us the general shape of the curve: it will start at a low pH (but not as low as a strong acid), have a buffer region, an equivalence point above pH 7, and level off at a high pH.

steps.title: Find the $pK_a$ landmark
steps.content:
To accurately anchor our sketch, we look up the $pK_a$ of ethanoic acid in the **IB Data Booklet** (typically Table 21). 

For ethanoic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$), the $pK_a$ is **4.76**. 

In a weak acid titration, the **half-equivalence point** (where half the acid has been neutralized) is a critical landmark because at this point:
$$\text{pH} = pK_a = 4.76$$

steps.title: Map the buffer region
steps.content:
The **buffer region** is the relatively flat part of the curve centered around the half-equivalence point. 

When sketching, ensure the curve rises gradually through the pH value of **4.76**. This area resists large changes in pH as NaOH is added, creating that characteristic 'plateau' shape before the vertical rise.

steps.title: Locate the equivalence point
steps.content:
The **equivalence point** occurs during the sharp vertical rise of the pH curve. 

Because we are titrating a weak acid with a strong base, the resulting salt (sodium ethanoate) undergoes hydrolysis, making the solution slightly basic. Therefore, you must place the equivalence point at a **pH > 7** (usually around 8.5–9.0).

steps.title: Sketch and label
steps.content:
Now, draw the curve following these landmarks:
1. Start at approximately **pH 3**.
2. Draw a gradual increase through the **buffer region** (pH ≈ 4.76).
3. Draw a sharp vertical section with the **equivalence point** labeled above pH 7.
4. Level off the curve at approximately **pH 13**.

Make sure to clearly label the 'buffer region' and 'equivalence point' as requested to secure both marks.

parts.content: Explain why a buffer solution forms during the titration and at what stage it occurs.
parts.markscheme:
- Buffer forms when excess weak acid and conjugate base (from partial neutralization) are present
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Occurs before the equivalence point, where both $\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$ and $\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$ exist in significant amounts
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the chemical reaction
steps.content:
To understand why a buffer forms, we first look at the neutralization reaction between the weak acid and the strong base:

$$\mathrm{CH_3COOH(aq) + NaOH(aq) \rightarrow CH_3COONa(aq) + H_2O(l)}$$

In this reaction, the hydroxide ions ($\mathrm{OH^-}$) from the strong base react with the ethanoic acid to produce the ethanoate ion ($\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$), which is the conjugate base of ethanoic acid.

steps.title: Analyze the stoichiometry
steps.content:
Before the equivalence point is reached, the amount of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ added is less than the initial amount of $\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$ present. 

Using the **Stoichiometric Neutralization Approach**, we treat $\mathrm{NaOH}$ as the **limiting reactant**. 

- Every mole of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ added reacts completely.
- This reaction consumes some of the weak acid and produces an equivalent amount of the conjugate base ($\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$).

steps.title: Explain the buffer formation
steps.content:
Because the strong base is the limiting reactant, the solution at this stage contains two key species:
1. **Unreacted weak acid** ($\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$)
2. **Produced conjugate base** ($\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$ from the salt $\mathrm{CH_3COONa}$)

A buffer is defined as a solution that contains significant amounts of both a weak acid and its conjugate base. This mixture is capable of resisting changes in pH when small amounts of acid or base are added.

highlights.text: Buffer forms when excess weak acid and conjugate base (from partial neutralization) are present

steps.title: State the timing
steps.content: This buffer region occurs **before the equivalence point**. Once the equivalence point is reached, all the $\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$ has been neutralized, and the solution no longer contains the weak acid needed to maintain the buffer system.

highlights.text: Occurs before the equivalence point, where both CH3COOH and CH3COO- exist in significant amounts

steps.title: Identify the chemical reaction
steps.content:
To understand why a buffer forms, we first look at the reaction between the weak acid, ethanoic acid, and the strong base, sodium hydroxide:

$$\mathrm{CH_3COOH(aq) + OH^-(aq) \rightarrow CH_3COO^-(aq) + H_2O(l)}$$

During the titration, as the $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is added, it reacts with the $\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$ to produce its conjugate base, the ethanoate ion ($\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$). This is a key prerequisite covered in **Topic R3.1: Proton transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Explain buffer formation
steps.content:
A buffer solution requires the presence of both a weak acid and its conjugate base. 

In this titration, as the strong base is added to the weak acid, only a portion of the acid is neutralized. This results in a mixture where the **excess weak acid** ($ \mathrm{CH_3COOH} $) and the **produced conjugate base** ($ \mathrm{CH_3COO^-} $) coexist in the same solution. 

This specific combination allows the solution to resist changes in pH, which is the defining characteristic of a buffer.

highlights.text: Buffer forms when excess weak acid and conjugate base (from partial neutralization) are present

steps.title: Identify the titration stage
steps.content:
Using the **Titration Curve Stage Approach**, we identify that this buffer region occurs **before the equivalence point**.

At any point before the equivalence point (but after the very start), the moles of base added are less than the initial moles of acid. Therefore:
1. Some $\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$ remains unreacted.
2. Some $\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$ has been formed.

On a pH curve, this corresponds to the "buffer region" where the gradient is relatively flat because the pH changes very slowly as more base is added.

highlights.text: Occurs before the equivalence point, where both CH3COOH and CH3COO- exist in significant amounts

options.markscheme: At equilibrium for a weak acid, dilution decreases $[\text{H}^+]$, so “increases/increases” is incorrect.
Dilution decreases $[\text{H}^+]$, but $n(\text{H}^+)= [\text{H}^+]\,V$ increases because the increase in $V$ outweighs the decrease in $[\text{H}^+]$ for a weak acid at equilibrium.

On dilution, formal concentration $C$ decreases by a factor of $4$. For a weak acid, $[\text{H}^+]\approx\sqrt{K_a C}$, so $[\text{H}^+]$ decreases (by factor $\sqrt{4}=2$). Then $n(\text{H}^+)=[\text{H}^+]V$ increases because $V$ increases by factor $4$ while $[\text{H}^+]$ decreases only by factor $2$.
At equilibrium for a weak acid, $[\text{H}^+]$ decreases on dilution, so “decreases/increases” is incorrect.

steps.title: Recall Brønsted–Lowry definitions
steps.content:
To identify acids and bases in a reaction, we use the Brønsted–Lowry theory. 

*   **Brønsted–Lowry Acid**: A species that **donates** a proton ($H^{+}$).
*   **Brønsted–Lowry Base**: A species that **accepts** a proton ($H^{+}$).

These definitions are fundamental to **Topic R3.1: Proton transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Analyze the ammonia (NH3)
steps.content:
Let's look at what happens to $\text{NH}_3$ as the reaction proceeds from left to right. 

$$\text{NH}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{NH}_4^+(aq)$$

Notice that $\text{NH}_3$ gains a hydrogen atom and its charge increases by $+1$. This means it has **accepted a proton** ($H^{+}$). Since it is a proton acceptor, $\text{NH}_3$ acts as the Brønsted–Lowry base.

steps.title: Analyze the water (H2O)
steps.content:
Now, let's look at what happens to $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ as it turns into products:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{OH}^-(aq)$$

In this process, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ loses a hydrogen atom and its charge decreases by $1$. This confirms that water has **donated a proton** ($H^{+}$) to the ammonia. Therefore, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ acts as the Brønsted–Lowry acid.

steps.title: Final identification
steps.content:
Based on our proton transfer analysis of the left-hand side of the equation:

*   **Brønsted–Lowry acid**: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (the proton donor)
*   **Brønsted–Lowry base**: $\text{NH}_3$ (the proton acceptor)

In the IB markscheme, you receive 1 mark for each correctly identified species.

steps.title: Recall Brønsted–Lowry definitions
steps.content:
According to the **Brønsted–Lowry theory** (covered in **Reactivity 3.1**), we define acids and bases by the movement of protons ($H^+$ ions):

- **Brønsted–Lowry Acid**: A species that **donates** a proton ($H^+$).
- **Brønsted–Lowry Base**: A species that **accepts** a proton ($H^+$).

steps.title: Link reactants to products
steps.content:
To use the **Conjugate Pair Identification** approach, we look at how the molecules change from the left side (reactants) to the right side (products):

1.  $\mathrm{NH_3}$ on the left becomes $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$ on the right.
2.  $\mathrm{H_2O}$ on the left becomes $\mathrm{OH^-}$ on the right.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Pair 1: } & \mathrm{NH_3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH_4^+} \\ \text{Pair 2: } & \mathrm{H_2O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{OH^-} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the proton acceptor
steps.content:
Let's look at the first pair: $\mathrm{NH_3} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH_4^+}$.

To become $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$, the ammonia molecule ($\mathrm{NH_3}$) must have **accepted** one hydrogen ion ($H^+$). Since it is a proton acceptor, $\mathrm{NH_3}$ acts as the **Brønsted–Lowry base**.

steps.title: Identify the proton donor
steps.content:
Now look at the second pair: $\mathrm{H_2O} \rightarrow \mathrm{OH^-}$.

To become the hydroxide ion ($\mathrm{OH^-}$), the water molecule ($\mathrm{H_2O}$) must have **donated** one hydrogen ion ($H^+$). Since it is a proton donor, $\mathrm{H_2O}$ acts as the **Brønsted–Lowry acid**.

steps.title: State the final identification
steps.content:
Based on our analysis of the proton transfer:

- **Brønsted–Lowry acid**: $\mathrm{H_2O}$
- **Brønsted–Lowry base**: $\mathrm{NH_3}$

In an IB exam, identifying each correctly would earn you one mark each for a total of 2 marks.

highlights.text: Identify the Brønsted–Lowry acid and base on the left-hand side of the equation.

parts.markscheme:
- Acid/base pair 1: $\mathrm{NH_3/NH_4^+}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Acid/base pair 2: $\mathrm{H_2O/OH^-}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the proton transfer
steps.content:
To identify conjugate acid–base pairs, we first look at how protons ($H^+$) move in the forward reaction. 

Comparing the reactants to the products:
- $\mathrm{NH_3}$ becomes $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$, meaning it has **accepted** a proton.
- $\mathrm{H_2O}$ becomes $\mathrm{OH^-}$, meaning it has **donated** a proton.

This behavior is described in **Topic R3.1: Proton transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Identify Brønsted–Lowry roles
steps.content:
According to the Brønsted–Lowry theory:
- An **acid** is a proton ($H^+$) donor.
- A **base** is a proton ($H^+$) acceptor.

In the forward reaction:
- $\mathrm{NH_3}$ acts as the **base** (it accepts $H^+$).
- $\mathrm{H_2O}$ acts as the **acid** (it donates $H^+$).

steps.title: Define conjugate pairs
steps.content:
A **conjugate acid–base pair** consists of two species that differ by only one proton ($H^+$). 

- When the base $\mathrm{NH_3}$ accepts a proton, it forms its conjugate acid, $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$.
- When the acid $\mathrm{H_2O}$ donates a proton, it forms its conjugate base, $\mathrm{OH^-}$.

steps.title: State the final pairs
steps.content:
The two conjugate acid–base pairs are:
1. $\mathrm{NH_3 / NH_4^+}$
2. $\mathrm{H_2O / OH^-}$

In an exam, you get 1 mark for correctly identifying each pair. You can write them as "acid/base" or just list them together as shown in the markscheme.

steps.title: Identify the criteria
steps.content: To identify conjugate acid–base pairs, we look for two chemical species that differ from each other by exactly one $\mathrm{H^+}$ ion (a proton). This is a core concept from **Topic R3.1: Proton transfer reactions**. We look for species with the same 'skeleton' but a slight difference in hydrogen count and charge.

highlights.text: State the conjugate acid–base pairs in the reaction.

steps.title: Compare nitrogen species
steps.content:
First, let's look at the species containing nitrogen on both sides of the equation:
- Reactant: $\mathrm{NH_3}$
- Product: $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$

Comparing these formulas, $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$ has one more hydrogen and a $+1$ charge compared to $\mathrm{NH_3}$. This confirms they differ by exactly one $\mathrm{H^+}$, making them the first conjugate pair.

steps.title: Compare oxygen species
steps.content:
Next, let's look at the species containing oxygen:
- Reactant: $\mathrm{H_2O}$
- Product: $\mathrm{OH^-}$

Comparing these, $\mathrm{H_2O}$ has one more hydrogen and a higher charge ($0$ vs $-1$) than $\mathrm{OH^-}$. Since they differ by exactly one $\mathrm{H^+}$, they form the second conjugate pair.

steps.title: Finalize the pairs
steps.content:
Based on our comparison of chemical formulas, the two conjugate acid–base pairs are:
- Pair 1: $\mathrm{NH_3/NH_4^+}$
- Pair 2: $\mathrm{H_2O/OH^-}$

In an IB exam, stating both pairs clearly will earn you both marks.

parts.content: Explain how this reaction supports the Brønsted–Lowry definition.
parts.markscheme:

- Base accepts a proton ($\mathrm{NH_3} \to \mathrm{NH_4^+}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define Brønsted–Lowry theory
steps.content:
To explain how this reaction fits the Brønsted–Lowry definition, we first need to recall the roles defined by this theory:

*   **Brønsted–Lowry Acid:** A species that donates a proton ($H^{+}$).
*   **Brønsted–Lowry Base:** A species that accepts a proton ($H^{+}$).

In this approach, we will compare the reactants to the products to see where the $H^{+}$ ion is moving.

steps.title: Analyze the water molecule
steps.content:
Let's look at what happens to water ($H_2O$) as it reacts. 

On the reactant side, we have $H_2O$. On the product side, it has turned into the hydroxide ion, $OH^{-}$. 

$$\text{Reactant: } H_2O \rightarrow \text{Product: } OH^{-}$$ 

By losing a hydrogen ion ($H^{+}$), the water molecule has acted as a **proton donor**. According to the definition, this makes water the **acid** in this reaction.

steps.title: Analyze the ammonia molecule
steps.content:
Now, let's look at ammonia ($NH_3$). 

On the reactant side, we have $NH_3$. On the product side, it has turned into the ammonium ion, $NH_4^{+}$.

$$\text{Reactant: } NH_3 \rightarrow \text{Product: } NH_4^{+}$$ 

By gaining a hydrogen ion ($H^{+}$), the ammonia molecule has acted as a **proton acceptor**. This identifies ammonia as the **base** in this reaction.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
The reaction supports the Brønsted–Lowry definition because it involves the transfer of a proton from $H_2O$ to $NH_3$. 

To secure both marks on an IB exam, you must explicitly state which species is donating and which is accepting the proton:

1.  **$H_2O$ is the acid** because it donates a proton ($H^{+}$) to form $OH^{-}$.
2.  **$NH_3$ is the base** because it accepts a proton ($H^{+}$) to form $NH_4^{+}$.

steps.title: Define Brønsted–Lowry theory
steps.content: According to the Brønsted–Lowry theory, an **acid** is a species that donates a proton ($H^+$), and a **base** is a species that accepts a proton. To explain how a reaction supports this definition, we look at how the molecules change as they transform into their conjugate partners.

steps.title: Identify the base partner
steps.content:
Let's look at ammonia, $\mathrm{NH_3}$. In the reaction, $\mathrm{NH_3}$ becomes the ammonium ion, $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$. 

$$\mathrm{NH_3} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH_4^+}$$

To make this change, the $\mathrm{NH_3}$ molecule must gain one $H^+$ ion. Because $\mathrm{NH_3}$ **accepts a proton**, it acts as a Brønsted–Lowry base. This is the first mark in the markscheme.

steps.title: Identify the acid partner
steps.content:
Now, let's look at water, $\mathrm{H_2O}$. In this reaction, $\mathrm{H_2O}$ becomes the hydroxide ion, $\mathrm{OH^-}$.

$$\mathrm{H_2O} \rightarrow \mathrm{OH^-}$$

To make this change, the $\mathrm{H_2O}$ molecule must lose one $H^+$ ion. Because $\mathrm{H_2O}$ **donates a proton** to the ammonia, it acts as a Brønsted–Lowry acid. This earns the second mark.

steps.title: Summary of proton transfer
steps.content:
In summary, the reaction supports the Brønsted–Lowry definition because it involves the transfer of a proton from water (the acid) to ammonia (the base), resulting in the formation of their conjugate pairs:

$$\underbrace{\mathrm{NH_3}}_{\text{base}} + \underbrace{\mathrm{H_2O}}_{\text{acid}} \rightleftharpoons \underbrace{\mathrm{NH_4^+}}_{\text{conjugate acid}} + \underbrace{\mathrm{OH^-}}_{\text{conjugate base}}$$

questions.specification: The chemistry of acids and bases involves proton transfer reactions that have significant implications for both biological systems and the conservation of cultural heritage.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. $\mathrm{KNO_2}$ gives a basic solution (conjugate base of weak acid $\mathrm{HNO_2}$), so it should have the highest pH, not the lowest.
Incorrect. $\mathrm{NH_4NO_3}$ is more acidic than $\mathrm{NH_4F}$ because $K_a(\mathrm{NH_4^+}) > K_b(\mathrm{F^-})$ (so $\mathrm{NH_4F}$ is only mildly acidic).
Incorrect. $\mathrm{KNO_3}$ is approximately neutral (pH $\approx 7$) and so cannot have the lowest pH compared with the ammonium salts; also $\mathrm{KNO_2}$ is more basic than $\mathrm{NH_4F}$.

Correct. $\mathrm{NH_4NO_3}$ is acidic due to $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$; $\mathrm{NH_4F}$ is mildly acidic since $K_a(\mathrm{NH_4^+}) > K_b(\mathrm{F^-})$; $\mathrm{KNO_3}$ is neutral (strong acid/strong base salt); and $\mathrm{KNO_2}$ is basic (conjugate base of weak acid $\mathrm{HNO_2}$).

A buffer is produced by mixing $30.0 \text{ cm}^3$ of $0.20 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ methanoic acid, $\text{HCOOH(aq)}$, with $0.20 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ potassium hydroxide, $\text{KOH(aq)}$.

What is the volume of $\text{KOH}$ required and the pH of the buffer?

| | Volume of $\text{KOH}$ / $\text{cm}^3$ | pH of buffer |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A** | $60.0$ | $10.2$ |
| **B** | $60.0$ | $3.8$ |
| **C** | $15.0$ | $10.2$ |
| **D** | $15.0$ | $3.8$ |

options.markscheme:
A buffer is formed when the weak acid is in excess and partially neutralized.

- Adding $60.0 \text{ cm}^3$ of $\text{KOH}$ would neutralize all the acid and leave an excess of strong base ($30.0 \text{ cm}^3$ excess), which does not constitute a buffer.
- Adding $15.0 \text{ cm}^3$ of $\text{KOH}$ neutralizes exactly half of the $30.0 \text{ cm}^3$ of methanoic acid. This reaches the half-equivalence point.

At the half-equivalence point:
- The concentrations of the weak acid and its conjugate base are equal: $[\text{HCOOH}] = [\text{HCOO}^-]$.
- Based on the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\left(\frac{[\text{base}]}{[\text{acid}]}\right)$, so $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$.
- Since $\text{p}K_a(\text{methanoic acid}) \approx 3.8$, the pH is $3.8$.

Therefore, Volume = $15.0 \text{ cm}^3$ and pH = $3.8$.

parts.content: Define the term pH.
parts.markscheme:
pH is the negative logarithm (base 10) of the hydrogen ion concentration:
$\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10} [{H^+}]$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the definition
steps.content: In chemistry, pH is a measure of the acidity or alkalinity of a solution. The most precise way to define it, as required by the IB markscheme, is through its mathematical relationship with the concentration of hydrogen ions.

steps.title: Use the Data Booklet
steps.content:
You can find the exact formula for pH in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your IB Data Booklet. This formula relates pH to the concentration of hydrogen ions, denoted as $[\text{H}^+]$.

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$$

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you should express the formula in words. The definition is: **pH is the negative logarithm (to the base 10) of the hydrogen ion concentration.**

Note that the square brackets in $[\text{H}^+]$ specifically represent concentration, usually measured in $\text{mol}\cdot\text{dm}^{-3}$.

highlights.text: pH is the negative logarithm (base 10) of the hydrogen ion concentration:

steps.title: Identify the requirement
steps.content: To define pH, we need to provide a precise scientific description of what this value represents in chemistry. This concept is a key part of **Topic R3.1: Proton transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Reference the formula
steps.content:
The Data Booklet provides the mathematical relationship used to calculate pH. We can use this equation to help construct our verbal definition:

$$\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10}[\mathrm{H}^{+}]$$

steps.title: Translate to verbal form
steps.content:
By looking at the formula, we can describe each component in words:

1. The $-$ sign indicates the **negative** value.
2. $\log_{10}$ represents the **logarithm to the base 10**.
3. The square brackets $[\dots]$ represent the **molar concentration** (measured in $\text{mol dm}^{-3}$).
4. $\text{H}^+$ represents **hydrogen ions**.

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
Combining these parts, the official IB definition for 1 mark is:

pH is the **negative logarithm (base 10)** of the **hydrogen ion concentration**.

parts.content: Calculate the pH of a solution with $[{H^+}] = 3.2 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}}$.
parts.markscheme:
- $\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10}(3.2 \times 10^{-4})$
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\mathrm{pH} = 3.49$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content: To solve this, we first identify the hydrogen ion concentration provided in the question. In IB Chemistry, this is covered in **Topic R3.1: Proton transfer reactions**, where we learn how concentration relates to the pH scale.

steps.title: Locate the pH formula
steps.content: Next, we look at the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of the IB Data Booklet to find the formula for pH. The formula shows that pH is the negative base-10 logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentration.

steps.title: Substitute the concentration
steps.content:
Now, substitute the given value $[H^+] = 3.2 \times 10^{-4}$ into the formula. This step shows your method and earns you the first mark.

$$\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10}(3.2 \times 10^{-4})$$

steps.title: Calculate the final pH
steps.content:
Finally, use your calculator to find the numerical value. In chemistry, pH is usually rounded to two decimal places.

$$\mathrm{pH} = 3.49$$

parts.content: Determine whether the solution is acidic, neutral, or basic.
parts.markscheme:
Acidic (pH < 7)
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define the neutral point
steps.content:
To determine the nature of the solution, we first establish the neutral reference point. At $298.15\text{ K}$ ($25^\circ\text{C}$), the ion product of water ($K_w$) is $1.00 \times 10^{-14}$. For a neutral solution where $[\text{H}^+] = [\text{OH}^-]$, the pH is exactly $7.0$.

This is a fundamental concept in **Topic R3.1: Proton transfer reactions**.

calculator.description: Verify the pH of a neutral solution at 298 K using the hydrogen ion concentration.

steps.title: Compare pH to 7
steps.content:
Using the **Comparing pH to 7** approach, we apply the following rules to classify the solution:

*   **Acidic:** $\text{pH} < 7$
*   **Neutral:** $\text{pH} = 7$
*   **Basic:** $\text{pH} > 7$

Based on the previous calculations or provided data, we identify that the pH of the solution falls into the acidic range.

steps.title: Conclude the solution nature
steps.content:
Since the calculated $\text{pH}$ is less than $7.0$, we conclude that the solution is **acidic**. 

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for correctly identifying the nature of the solution as acidic by referencing the pH value relative to 7.

highlights.text: Determine whether the solution is acidic, neutral, or basic.

steps.title: Define the neutral point
steps.content:
To determine if a solution is acidic, neutral, or basic using ion concentrations, we first look at the self-ionization of water at $298.15\text{ K}$. According to the **Physical Constants** section of your data booklet:

$$K_{\text{w}} = [\text{H}^{+}][\text{OH}^{-}] = 1.00 \times 10^{-14} \text{ mol}^{2} \text{ dm}^{-6}$$

In a perfectly **neutral** solution, the concentration of hydrogen ions is equal to the concentration of hydroxide ions:

$$[\text{H}^{+}] = [\text{OH}^{-}] = \sqrt{1.00 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.00 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$$

steps.title: Compare ion concentrations
steps.content:
Using the **Comparing Ion Concentrations** approach, we classify the solution based on the relative amounts of $[\text{H}^{+}]$ and $[\text{OH}^{-}]$:

*   **Acidic:** $[\text{H}^{+}] > [\text{OH}^{-}]$ (which means $[\text{H}^{+}] > 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$)
*   **Neutral:** $[\text{H}^{+}] = [\text{OH}^{-}]$
*   **Basic:** $[\text{H}^{+}] < [\text{OH}^{-}]$ (which means $[\text{H}^{+}] < 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$)

steps.title: State the final classification
steps.content:
Since the markscheme identifies the solution as acidic, we know that the concentration of hydrogen ions $[\text{H}^{+}]$ is greater than the concentration of hydroxide ions $[\text{OH}^{-}]$. This corresponds to a $\text{pH}$ value of less than $7$.

Therefore, the solution is **acidic**.

steps.title: Identify the chemical species
steps.content: To determine the nature of the solution using **Chemical Identity Analysis**, we first look at the solute involved. Based on the previous parts of the question, we are dealing with a substance that increases the concentration of hydrogen ions $[H^+]$ in the water.

steps.title: Analyze the solute's properties
steps.content:
An acidic solution is formed when the solute is either a strong/weak acid or a salt that undergoes cation hydrolysis (like an ammonium salt). These species release protons into the solution:

$$\text{HA (aq)} \rightarrow \text{H}^+ \text{(aq)} + \text{A}^- \text{(aq)}$$

Because the substance identified in the problem produces an excess of $[H^+]$ ions, we classify it as an acid.

steps.title: Determine the pH category
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, the nature of a solution at $298 \text{ K}$ is defined by its pH value relative to 7:
- **Acidic:** $\text{pH} < 7$
- **Neutral:** $\text{pH} = 7$
- **Basic:** $\text{pH} > 7$

Since our chemical identity analysis identifies the solute as an acid, the resulting solution must be acidic.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: The solution is **acidic** because the solute's chemical identity is that of an acid, leading to a $\text{pH}$ less than 7.

questions.specification:
$NH_3(aq) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons NH_4^+(aq) + OH^-(aq)$.

$NH_3$ and $NH_4^+$ are a conjugate acid–base pair. Which particle differs between a conjugate acid–base pair?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.1 Proton transfer reactions > R3.1.2 Conjugate acid-base pairs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.1.2 Conjugate acid-base pairs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks what species differs between a conjugate acid–base pair (NH3/NH4+), which is defined by gain/loss of a proton (H+).

options.markscheme: $OH^-$ is produced in the equilibrium but is not the particle that distinguishes $NH_3$ from $NH_4^+$ in a conjugate pair.
Conjugate acid–base pairs differ by one proton, $H^+$; $NH_4^+$ has one more $H^+$ than $NH_3$.
Conjugate acid–base pairs differ by a proton, not an electron.
The difference is specifically a proton, $H^+$, not a neutral hydrogen atom.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.1.16 Buffer solutions (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which mixtures of an acid/base will produce a buffer solution, i.e., a weak acid with its conjugate base (or weak base with conjugate acid) often formed via partial neutralization.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.1.7 Neutralization reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Determining buffer formation here relies on neutralization/stoichiometry (whether the weak acid is partially neutralized by the strong base), so neutralization reactions are directly involved.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.1 How much? The amount of chemical change > R2.1.3 Limiting and excess reactants
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.1.3 Limiting and excess reactants
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The options require identifying excess vs limiting reactant (e.g., whether KOH fully neutralizes the weak acid or leaves both HA and A− present), which is a limiting/excess reactants concept.

options.content: $20\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ HCl(aq)}$ and $20\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ KOH(aq)}$
options.markscheme: The amount of strong acid ($\text{HCl}$) is equal to the amount of strong base ($\text{KOH}$). This results in a neutral salt solution ($\text{KCl}$), which has no buffering capacity.

options.content: $40\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ C}_2\text{H}_5\text{COOH(aq)}$ and $40\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ KOH(aq)}$
options.markscheme: The amount of weak acid ($\text{propanoic acid}$) is exactly equal to the amount of strong base ($\text{KOH}$). The weak acid is completely converted to its salt ($\text{potassium propanoate}$), so no weak acid remains to form a buffer system.

options.content: $20\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ C}_2\text{H}_5\text{COOH(aq)}$ and $40\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ KOH(aq)}$
options.markscheme: The amount of strong base ($\text{KOH}$) is in excess compared to the weak acid. All the weak acid is consumed, leaving a solution of strong base and salt, which is not a buffer.

options.content: $40\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ C}_2\text{H}_5\text{COOH(aq)}$ and $20\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ KOH(aq)}$
options.markscheme: There is an excess of weak acid ($0.004\text{ mol}$) compared to the strong base ($0.002\text{ mol}$). The partial neutralization produces $0.002\text{ mol}$ of the conjugate base ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{COO}^-$) and leaves $0.002\text{ mol}$ of unreacted weak acid. This mixture of a weak acid and its conjugate base constitutes a buffer solution.

questions.specification:
Which pair(s) of oxides, when each oxide is assumed to react completely with water, produce an acidic aqueous solution (pH less than 7.0) at $298\text{ K}$?

$\mathrm{Li_2O}$ and $\mathrm{MgO}$

$\mathrm{SO_3}$ and $\mathrm{P_4O_{10}}$

$\mathrm{Cl_2O_7}$ and $\mathrm{N_2O_5}$

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 3. Classification of matter > S3.1 The periodic table: Classification of elements > S3.1.5 Metallic and non-metallic oxides
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S3.1.5 Metallic and non-metallic oxides
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which oxides form acidic solutions (pH<7) on reaction with water, distinguishing metal oxides (basic) from non-metal oxides (acidic). This directly tests classification/behavior of metallic vs non-metallic oxides.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.1 Proton transfer reactions > R3.1.6 Strong and weak acids and bases
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.1.6 Strong and weak acids and bases
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Identifying pH<7 products requires recognizing that certain oxides are acid anhydrides that form strong acids in water (e.g., SO3→H2SO4, N2O5→HNO3), linking to strong vs weak acids/bases.

options.markscheme: $\mathrm{Li_2O}$ and $\mathrm{MgO}$ are basic oxides that form alkaline solutions (pH > 7) when reacted with water.
$\mathrm{Li_2O}$ and $\mathrm{MgO}$ produce basic solutions, while the oxides in II produce acidic solutions.

Oxides in II form sulfuric acid ($\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$) and phosphoric acid ($\mathrm{H_3PO_4}$). Oxides in III form perchloric acid ($\mathrm{HClO_4}$) and nitric acid ($\mathrm{HNO_3}$). All of these result in a pH < 7.
Group I consists of basic oxides, which increase the pH rather than decreasing it.

questions.specification: Which of $\text{H}_2\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^-$, $\text{HC}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^{2-}$ and $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^{3-}$ are amphiprotic?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.1 Proton transfer reactions > R3.1.3 Amphiprotic species
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.1.3 Amphiprotic species
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which successive dissociation products of citric acid are amphiprotic (can both donate and accept H+), directly testing identification of amphiprotic species in acid–base equilibria.

options.markscheme: $\text{HC}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^{2-}$ is amphiprotic, but $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^{3-}$ cannot donate a proton (no H), so it is not amphiprotic.

Both ions can donate $\text{H}^+$ (act as acids) and accept $\text{H}^+$ (act as bases): $\text{H}_2\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7$ or $\text{HC}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^{2-}$; $\text{HC}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^-$ or $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^{3-}$.
$\text{H}_2\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^-$ is amphiprotic, but $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^{3-}$ cannot donate a proton (no H), so it is not amphiprotic.
$\text{HC}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^{2-}$ is amphiprotic, but $\text{H}_2\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7^-$ is also amphiprotic because it can both donate and accept $\text{H}^+$.

questions.specification: Environmental scientists monitoring a lake near a heavily industrialized region have recorded a significant decrease in the water's pH. This is attributed mainly to sulfuric acid deposition formed from atmospheric oxidation of a gaseous pollutant. Which gaseous pollutant is primarily responsible?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.1 Proton transfer reactions > R3.1.4 pH and the hydrogen ion concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.1.4 pH and the hydrogen ion concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario describes a decrease in lake water pH due to acid deposition, linking to understanding pH and hydrogen ion concentration changes in environmental contexts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 3. Classification of matter > S3.1 The periodic table: Classification of elements > S3.1.5 Metallic and non-metallic oxides
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S3.1.5 Metallic and non-metallic oxides
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Acid deposition is caused by acidic (non-metal) oxides such as nitrogen oxides forming acids in water; the options include NO2, a non-metal oxide relevant to this concept.

options.markscheme: Nitrogen dioxide can contribute to acid deposition by forming nitric acid, but it is not the primary source of sulfuric acid deposition.
Sulfur dioxide is oxidized in the atmosphere and forms sulfuric acid in rainwater, making it the primary cause of sulfuric acid deposition near industrial sources.
Carbon monoxide does not react with water to form strong acids and is not a primary contributor to acid deposition.
Nitrous oxide is a greenhouse gas but is not a primary precursor to acids responsible for acid deposition.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Nucleophiles are electron pair donors.
Incorrect. Brønsted–Lowry bases are proton acceptors; electrophiles are electron pair acceptors (Lewis acids).
Correct. Nucleophiles donate a pair of electrons to form a new covalent bond, which is the definition of a Lewis base.
Incorrect. Electrophiles accept electron pairs and are therefore Lewis acids.

questions.specification: Which of the following salts, when dissolved in distilled water to form a $0.10\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}$ solution at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$, produces a solution with $[\mathrm{H^+}] < 1.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}$ (pH $>7$)? Assume ideal behavior.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.1.12 The pH of a salt solution (Higher Level only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which salt produces [H+] < 1.0×10^-7 (i.e., basic pH) upon dissolving in water, which is determined by salt hydrolysis and conjugate acid/base effects on pH of salt solutions.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.1.5 The ion product of water (Kw)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the reference point [H+]=1.0×10^-7 at 25°C (neutral water via Kw), so understanding the ion product of water underpins the criterion for basic vs acidic solutions.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.1.4 pH and the hydrogen ion concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Interpreting a condition on hydrogen ion concentration relates directly to pH/[H+] relationships (pH > 7 corresponds to [H+] < 10^-7).

options.markscheme: $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$ is the conjugate acid of a weak base ($\mathrm{NH_3}$), so the solution is acidic ($[\mathrm{H^+}]>1.0\times 10^{-7}$).

Sodium acetate is a salt derived from a strong base ($\mathrm{NaOH}$) and a weak acid ($\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$). In aqueous solution, the acetate ion undergoes hydrolysis to produce hydroxide ions:

$$\mathrm{CH_3COO^- (aq)} + \mathrm{H_2O (l)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH_3COOH (aq)} + \mathrm{OH^- (aq)}$$

This increases $[\mathrm{OH^-}]$, making the solution basic so $[\mathrm{H^+}] < 1.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}$ (pH $>7$).
$\mathrm{Al^{3+}}$ undergoes hydrolysis to produce $\mathrm{H^+}$, so the solution is acidic ($[\mathrm{H^+}]>1.0\times 10^{-7}$).
$\mathrm{Cu^{2+}}$ undergoes hydrolysis to produce $\mathrm{H^+}$, so the solution is acidic ($[\mathrm{H^+}]>1.0\times 10^{-7}$).

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Diluting an acid reduces $[\text{H}^+]$ but here the dilution factor is $100$, giving $\text{pH}=4$, not $6$.

Initial $\text{pH} = 2 \Rightarrow [\text{H}^+] = 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
Final volume $= 25.0 + 2475.0 = 2500.0 \text{ cm}^3$
Dilution factor $= \frac{2500.0}{25.0} = 100$
Final $[\text{H}^+] = \frac{10^{-2}}{100} = 10^{-4} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
Final $\text{pH} = 4$
Incorrect. This would be the pH if no dilution occurred; dilution decreases $[\text{H}^+]$ and increases pH.
Incorrect. A pH of $3$ would correspond to a tenfold dilution; the actual dilution factor is $100$, giving $\text{pH}=4$.

questions.specification:
The table below shows the acid dissociation constants, $$K_a$$, for four weak monoprotic acids at $$298 \text{ K}$$.

| Acid | $$K_a$$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| $$HX$$ | $$1.2 \times 10^{-2}$$ |
| $$HY$$ | $$1.8 \times 10^{-5}$$ |
| $$HZ$$ | $$6.3 \times 10^{-8}$$ |
| $$HW$$ | $$4.8 \times 10^{-11}$$ |

Which $$0.10 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$$ aqueous solution of the following sodium salts will have the highest pH?
